From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Genre of Italian crime films

"Euro crime" redirects here. For supranational criminal law of the EU, see [European crimes](/wiki/European_crimes "European crimes").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Milano_calibro_9_%28Ugo_Piazza%29.PNG/250px-Milano_calibro_9_%28Ugo_Piazza%29.PNG)](/wiki/File:Milano_calibro_9_(Ugo_Piazza).PNG)

[Gastone Moschin](/wiki/Gastone_Moschin "Gastone Moschin") in *[Caliber 9](/wiki/Caliber_9 "Caliber 9")* (1972), directed by [Fernando Di Leo](/wiki/Fernando_Di_Leo "Fernando Di Leo")

***Poliziotteschi*** (Italian: [[polittsjotˈteski]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); sg.: ***poliziottesco***) constitute a subgenre of [crime](/wiki/Crime_film "Crime film") and [action films](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") that emerged in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") in the late 1960s and reached the height of their popularity in the 1970s.[[1]](#cite_note-indiewire.com-1) They are also known as ***polizieschi all'italiana***, ***Italo-crime***, **spaghetti crime films**, or simply **Italian crime films**. Influenced primarily by both 1970s [French crime films](/wiki/Gangster_film#French_gangster_films "Gangster film") and gritty 1960s and 1970s American [cop films](/wiki/Crime_thriller "Crime thriller") and [vigilante films](/wiki/Vigilante_film "Vigilante film") (among other influences),[[2]](#cite_note-birthmoviesdeath.com-2) *poliziotteschi* films were made amidst an atmosphere of socio-political turmoil in Italy known as [Years of Lead](/wiki/Years_of_Lead_(Italy) "Years of Lead (Italy)") and amidst increasing Italian crime rates. The films generally featured graphic and brutal violence, organized crime, [car chases](/wiki/Car_chase "Car chase"), vigilantism, [heists](/wiki/Heist_film "Heist film"), gunfights, and corruption up to the highest levels. The protagonists were generally tough [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") loners, willing to act outside a corrupt or overly bureaucratic system.[[3]](#cite_note-curti-3)

## Etymology of the noun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poliziotteschi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology of the noun")]

In Italian, *poliziesco* is the grammatically correct Italian adjective (resulting from the fusion of the noun *polizia*, "police", and the [desinence](/wiki/Desinence "Desinence") *-esco*, "related to", akin to the English "-esque") for police-related dramas, ranging from [Ed McBain](/wiki/Ed_McBain "Ed McBain")'s [police procedural](/wiki/Police_procedural "Police procedural") novels to [forensic science](/wiki/Forensic_science "Forensic science") investigations. In Italian media, *poliziesco* is used generally to indicate every [detective fiction](/wiki/Detective_fiction "Detective fiction") production where police forces (Italian or foreign) are the main protagonists.

In English, the term *poliziottesco* (a fusion of the words *poliziotto* ("policeman") and the same *-esco* suffix) has prevailed over the more syntactically correct *poliziesco all'italiana* to indicate 1970s-era Italian-produced "tough cop" and crime movies. The prevalence of *poliziottesco* over poliziesco [all'italiana] closely follows the success of the term spaghetti Western over *western all'italiana*, being shorter and more vivid. In both instances, however, the term that has come to be used more frequently by English-speaking fans of the genre (*poliziotteschi*, spaghetti Westerns) was originally used pejoratively by critics, to denigrate the films themselves and their makers.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poliziotteschi&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Banditi_a_Milano.JPG/250px-Banditi_a_Milano.JPG)](/wiki/File:Banditi_a_Milano.JPG)

*[Bandits in Milan](/wiki/Bandits_in_Milan "Bandits in Milan")* by [Carlo Lizzani](/wiki/Carlo_Lizzani "Carlo Lizzani") (1968)

Although the subgenre has its roots in Italian [heist films](/wiki/Heist_film "Heist film") of the late 1960s, such as *[Bandits in Milan](/wiki/Bandits_in_Milan "Bandits in Milan")* (*Banditi a Milano*, 1968) by [Carlo Lizzani](/wiki/Carlo_Lizzani "Carlo Lizzani"), it was also strongly influenced by such rough-edged American police thrillers of the late 1960s and early 1970s as *[Bullitt](/wiki/Bullitt "Bullitt")*, *[Dirty Harry](/wiki/Dirty_Harry "Dirty Harry")*, *[The French Connection](/wiki/The_French_Connection_(film) "The French Connection (film)")*, *[Magnum Force](/wiki/Magnum_Force "Magnum Force")*, and *[Serpico](/wiki/Serpico "Serpico")*; the 1970s wave of American [vigilante films](/wiki/Vigilante_film "Vigilante film"), including 1974's *[Death Wish](/wiki/Death_Wish_(1974_film) "Death Wish (1974 film)")*; the increase of cynicism and violence in [French](/wiki/French_cinema "French cinema") crime films; the resurgence of [mob films](/wiki/Mob_film "Mob film") in the wake of *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather")*; French and American [noir](/wiki/Noir_film "Noir film") and [neo-noir](/wiki/Neo-noir "Neo-noir") films; and the rise of [exploitation films](/wiki/Exploitation_films "Exploitation films") in the late 1960s and 1970s. More generally, the genre was also heavily influenced by [real-life crime and unrest in 1970s Italy](/wiki/Years_of_Lead_(Italy) "Years of Lead (Italy)") during the period known as the *anni di piombo* (political violence, kidnappings, assassinations, bank robberies, political militant terrorism, [impending oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis"), political corruption, organized crime-related violence, and recession).[[2]](#cite_note-birthmoviesdeath.com-2)

Just as American police films, American crime thrillers, and American vigilante films of the time focused on the crime waves and urban decline in the United States of the 1960s and 1970s, *poliziotteschi* were set in the context of, or directly addressed, the sociopolitical tumult and violence of Italy's *[anni di piombo](/wiki/Years_of_Lead_(Italy) "Years of Lead (Italy)")*, or the "Years of Lead", a period of widespread social unrest, political upheaval, labour unrest, rising crime, political violence, and political terrorism from the 1960s to 1980s. During this period, paramilitary and militant political terrorist groups, both on the far left (e.g. the [Red Brigades](/wiki/Red_Brigades "Red Brigades")) and far right (e.g. the [neo-fascist](/wiki/Neo-fascist "Neo-fascist") [Nuclei Armati Rivoluzionari](/wiki/Nuclei_Armati_Rivoluzionari "Nuclei Armati Rivoluzionari")) engaged in kidnappings, assassinations, and bombings (such as the [Piazza Fontana bombing](/wiki/Piazza_Fontana_bombing "Piazza Fontana bombing"), the murder of former prime minister [Aldo Moro](/wiki/Aldo_Moro "Aldo Moro"), and [1980 Bologna train station bombing](/wiki/Bologna_massacre "Bologna massacre")). At the same time, there was a period of especially violent conflict and disorder within the [Sicilian Mafia](/wiki/Sicilian_Mafia "Sicilian Mafia"), kicked off with the "[First Mafia War](/wiki/First_Mafia_War "First Mafia War")" of the 1960s and culminating in the "[Second Mafia War](/wiki/Second_Mafia_War "Second Mafia War")" of the early 1980s. Italian organized crime groups such as the Sicilian Mafia, the [Camorra](/wiki/Camorra "Camorra"), and especially the Roman [Banda della Magliana](/wiki/Banda_della_Magliana "Banda della Magliana") were actively involved in both criminal and political activities during this time, carrying out bombings and kidnappings, making deals with corrupt politicians, and forming strong ties to extreme far-right groups and neo-fascist terrorist organizations. Accordingly, *poliziotteschi* films such as *[Execution Squad](/wiki/Execution_Squad "Execution Squad")* (1972) often featured political extremists and paramilitary or terrorist groups alongside or in addition to the more commonly featured apolitical *[mafiosi](/wiki/Mafiosi "Mafiosi")* and [gangster](/wiki/Gangster "Gangster") criminal elements found in Italian crime films.[[3]](#cite_note-curti-3)[[4]](#cite_note-bayman-4)[[5]](#cite_note-aulenti-5)[[6]](#cite_note-bondanella-6)[[7]](#cite_note-directory-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Romano_puppo.jpg/250px-Romano_puppo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Romano_puppo.jpg)

*[The Big Racket](/wiki/The_Big_Racket "The Big Racket")* by [Enzo G. Castellari](/wiki/Enzo_G._Castellari "Enzo G. Castellari") (1976)

Due in part to the genre's often ostensibly negative portrayal of political activists and militants, especially leftist militants, and its seeming endorsement of [vigilantism](/wiki/Vigilantism "Vigilantism") and "tough-on-crime" or "[law and order](/wiki/Law_and_order_(politics) "Law and order (politics)")" stances, some *poliziotteschi* films (such as 1976's *[The Big Racket](/wiki/The_Big_Racket "The Big Racket")*) were criticized by then-contemporaneous critics and accused of exploiting [conservative](/wiki/Conservative "Conservative") fears of rising crime and political upheaval while containing [reactionary](/wiki/Reactionary "Reactionary"), pro-violence, or even quasi-[Fascist](/wiki/Italian_Fascism "Italian Fascism") ideological elements in their overarching message. These critiques were similar to those levelled at the 1970s American "[vigilante films](/wiki/Vigilante_film "Vigilante film")" of the same period, such as 1974's *[Death Wish](/wiki/Death_Wish_(1974_film) "Death Wish (1974 film)")*, films by which the *poliziotteschi* genre was considerably influenced.[[3]](#cite_note-curti-3)[[4]](#cite_note-bayman-4)[[6]](#cite_note-bondanella-6)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In retrospect, despite contemporaneous claims in the 1970s of overly conservative or reactionary themes within the genre, film historians such as Louis Bayman and Peter Bondarella contend that, in fact, *poliziotteschi* films generally presented a more multi-faceted, complex outlook on the political turmoil and crime waves of the time, as well as violence in general, with Bayman and author Roberto Curti in particular arguing that the genre generally used political conflicts and violence for largely [apolitical](/wiki/Apolitical "Apolitical") [tension-building](/wiki/Suspense "Suspense") and [cathartic](/wiki/Catharsis "Catharsis") or emotional purposes rather than to promote any particular political agendas.[[3]](#cite_note-curti-3)[[10]](#cite_note-destrasinistra-10) Curti notes that the genre's protagonists often simultaneously displayed both right-wing and left-wing views, and protagonists were often [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") while villains were often wealthy right-wing conservatives.[[3]](#cite_note-curti-3) The film *[Caliber 9](/wiki/Caliber_9 "Caliber 9")* (1972), for instance, features protagonists of both right-wing and leftist ideologies and offers differing views on the causes of crime and the true antagonists of law-abiding Italian society, while *Execution Squad* reveals the actual antagonists of the film to be right-wing reactionary, "tough-on-crime" ex-police officers and vigilantes rather than the initially suspected leftist militants.[[4]](#cite_note-bayman-4)[[6]](#cite_note-bondanella-6) Rather than explicitly supporting violence or vigilantism, the genre just as often displayed a morally ambiguous or aloof position on these themes, or even presented vigilantism and violence as a *[no-win situation](/wiki/No-win_situation "No-win situation").* Though *poliziotteschi* films have been viewed by some critics as condemning a "liberal" or "weak" judiciary system as ineffectual in its treatment of criminals, the genre also suggests a more general distrust of authority, whether left-wing or right-wing, by portraying right-wing law enforcement, politicians and businessmen as hopelessly corrupt and manipulative.[[3]](#cite_note-curti-3)[[4]](#cite_note-bayman-4) According to Bondarella, the "classic" *poliziotteschi* film reveals "almost universal suspicion of the very social institutions charged with protecting Italian society from criminal violence".[[6]](#cite_note-bondanella-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/La_polizia_chiede_aiuto.png/250px-La_polizia_chiede_aiuto.png)](/wiki/File:La_polizia_chiede_aiuto.png)

*[What Have They Done to Your Daughters?](/wiki/What_Have_They_Done_to_Your_Daughters%3F "What Have They Done to Your Daughters?")* by [Massimo Dallamano](/wiki/Massimo_Dallamano "Massimo Dallamano") (1974)

With directors such as [Fernando Di Leo](/wiki/Fernando_Di_Leo "Fernando Di Leo") and [Umberto Lenzi](/wiki/Umberto_Lenzi "Umberto Lenzi") and actors such as [Maurizio Merli](/wiki/Maurizio_Merli "Maurizio Merli") and [Tomas Milian](/wiki/Tomas_Milian "Tomas Milian"), *poliziotteschi* films became popular in the mid-1970s after the decline of [Spaghetti Westerns](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western") and [Eurospy](/wiki/Eurospy "Eurospy") genres. The subgenre lost its mainstream popularity in the late 1970s as [Italian erotic comedy](/wiki/Commedia_sexy_all%27italiana "Commedia sexy all'italiana") and horror films started topping the Italian box office.

Although based around crime and detective work, *poliziotteschi* should not be confused with the other popular Italian crime genre of the 1970s, the *[giallo](/wiki/Giallo "Giallo")*, which, to English-speaking and non-Italian audiences, refers to a genre of violent Italian murder-mystery thriller-horror films. Directors and stars often moved between both forms, and some films could be considered under either banner, such as [Massimo Dallamano](/wiki/Massimo_Dallamano "Massimo Dallamano")'s *[What Have They Done to Your Daughters?](/wiki/What_Have_They_Done_to_Your_Daughters%3F "What Have They Done to Your Daughters?")* (1974).

The *poliziottesco* subgenre gradually declined in popularity during the late 1970s. Screenwriter [Dardano Sacchetti](/wiki/Dardano_Sacchetti "Dardano Sacchetti"), who was unhappy with what he deemed the genre's "fascistic" undertones, credits himself for "destroying it from the inside", by making it evolve into self-[parody](/wiki/Parody "Parody"). By the end of the decade, the most successful films associated with the genre were [crime](/wiki/Crime_films "Crime films")-[comedy](/wiki/Comedy_films "Comedy films") pictures, which gradually evolved towards pure comedy.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
